title: Ponte Santa Trinita temporary restoration, 1944
highlights:
Trinita, located downstream of Ponte Vecchio in Firenze, Italy, was built between 1567 to 1569 by Florentine architect Bartolomeo Ammannati. The bridge was destroyed by retreating Wehrmacht forces on the night of August 3rd 1944, as the advancing British 8th Army pressured the Germans to leave the city after a year-long occupation. In the image we can see a temporary Bailey Bridge built by Royal Engineers, after British forces liberated Firenze. title: Santa Trinita Bridge - Firenze-Oltrarno.net
highlights: the bridge joins Via Tornabuoni and Via Maggio. the Bridge a Santa Trinita is a real masterpiece of Renaissance architecture; it is often called «the most beautiful  ...    In 1252 the noblesman Lamberto Frescobaldi sponsored the building of a new wooden bridge to join the important Via  ...    elegant line and to the completely new design of the arches. In 1608, in order to celebrate the wedding of Cosimo II  ...    Click to enlarge pictures. In august 1944 the bridge was destroyed again by mines laid by the retreating German army. In 1948 the City decided to rebuild the bridge «as it was and where it stood», instead of replace it with a modern bridge as proposed from some art historians. Under direction of architect Gizdulich and engineer Brizzi the building begun only in 1955, after many disputes and long researches in order to resolve the puzzle of the arches, whose curve proved to be very difficult to calculate. For the reconstruction almost all original stones were recovered

title: Ponte Santa Trinita

Come gli altri ponti fiorentini, ad eccezione del Ponte Vecchio, fu minato e distrutto dalle truppe tedesche in ritirata la notte del 3 agosto 1944. title: Ponti | Memorie di Resistenza fiorentina
highlights: distruzione dei ponti di Firenze da parte dei tedeschi nella notte tra il 3 e il 4 agosto 1944 aveva lo scopo di  ...    Dalle 22 del 3 agosto fino all'alba del 4 agosto 1944 una serie d'esplosioni, una dopo l'altra, distrusse i ponti, come un effetto domino: Ponte alle Grazie, Ponte Santa Trinita, Ponte alla Carraia, Ponte San Niccolò, alla Vittoria, e tutte le strade intorno a Ponte Vecchio, l'unico a rimanere in piedi, per decisione degli stessi nazisti.
